report: The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks of the right arm and leg, with the left arm remaining relatively still. The jerks progress to involve the entire right side of the body, while the left side shows minimal movement. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.
The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by right arm flexion and rightward head version. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of the limbs.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are synchronous and generalized, progressing to involve the entire body with generalized tonic-clonic activity, and there is no clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient is lying supine with a bandage on the head, holding a phone in the left hand and resting the right hand on the abdomen. There is no observable blank stare, head version, unilateral arm movement, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. The patient remains motionless throughout the video, with no signs of automatisms or asynchronous shaking.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements involving the limbs and trunk, which are synchronous and persist throughout the recording with no clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive during the event. In another segment, no seizure activity is observed, with the patient remaining motionless and eyes closed throughout, showing no visible signs of automatisms, clonic jerks, or tonic stiffening.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are synchronous and generalized, with no clear unilateral predominance. However, at other times, no seizure activity is observed; the patient remains motionless with eyes closed, showing no signs of automatisms, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks throughout the recording.

The patient exhibits a blank stare and lip smacking, followed by tonic stiffening involving the upper body and head, with bilateral clonic jerks affecting the arms and legs. The movements are initially asynchronous but become synchronous and generalized, persisting throughout the episode without clear unilateral predominance.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by unresponsiveness throughout the episode. In some instances, this is associated with subtle automatisms such as lip smacking or eyes closed without movement. The blank stare may progress to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso, involving the entire body. These movements are typically asynchronous and without clear unilateral predominance. In certain segments, the seizure begins with unilateral features, including right arm flexion with left extension (fencer’s posture) or head version to the right, sometimes evolving into generalized bilateral tonic–clonic activity. One segment shows clonic jerks initially localized to the right arm and leg, progressing to involve the entire right side with minimal left-sided involvement. Clonic components may include facial grimacing and involve the head and upper limbs bilaterally but asynchronously. Tonic stiffening of the upper body and limbs may precede bilateral clonic jerks. Episodes consistently conclude with the patient becoming unresponsive and lying still.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face, arms, and legs. The movements are synchronous and generalized, with no clear unilateral predominance.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks involving the face and upper extremities. The movements are synchronous and generalized, with no clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic jerking of the right arm and leg, progressing to bilateral tonic-clonic movements. The jerking is asynchronous, with the right side more prominently involved, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic clonic jerks initially involving the right arm and leg, which then spread to the left arm and leg, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. In some episodes, the clonic jerks are bilateral and asynchronous from onset, without clear unilateral predominance. In other instances, the episode begins with tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, evolving into bilateral asynchronous clonic jerks. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all events, which conclude with gradual relaxation and persistent unresponsiveness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare accompanied by subtle lip smacking, followed by right arm flexion and left arm extension in a fencer-like posture. This progresses to tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, then evolves into bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The patient becomes unresponsive during the event, which is consistently observed across multiple episodes.

The patient begins with a blank stare and lip smacking, which progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking of the limbs, head, and torso. This is accompanied by tonic stiffening and evolves into fencer posturing with left arm flexion and right arm extension.
Blank stare, then bilateral tonic–clonic movements with arms flexing and extending. Jerking spreads symmetrically, followed by postictal relaxation and unresponsiveness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by one of the following motor patterns: (1) rightward head version and right arm flexion or extension, evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking, sometimes progressing to fencer posturing (left flexion with right extension); (2) tonic stiffening of the upper body with synchronous bilateral clonic jerks involving arms and legs, without clear unilateral predominance; or (3) bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs progressing to asynchronous, irregular clonic jerks without a consistent pattern of fencer posturing or unilateral involvement. In all observed episodes, the patient remains unresponsive. One observation noted no clear seizure activity, with the patient remaining unresponsive, eyes closed, and without automatisms, clonic jerks, or tonic stiffening.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements, beginning with tonic stiffening of the limbs and clenched fists, progressing to synchronous, rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms, legs, and head. In some episodes, the seizure initiates with rightward head version and right arm extension (fencer’s posture) or right arm flexion with left arm extension, preceding the onset of bilateral tonic–clonic activity. The motor manifestations are generalized and without clear unilateral predominance in most cases, though initial lateralizing signs are noted. Lip smacking may occur subtly at the onset. The seizures evolve into full-body rhythmic jerking with synchronous involvement of limbs and torso, culminating in postictal unresponsiveness and generalized flaccidity. The patient remains unresponsive throughout all phases of the event.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by motor manifestations that vary across episodes. In several instances, the episode begins with right arm flexion and left arm extension in a fencing posture, followed by rhythmic clonic jerks and unresponsiveness at the end. Other episodes involve bilateral rhythmic clonic jerks affecting the face and upper limbs, which are either synchronous or asynchronous, without clear unilateral predominance. One episode features predominantly right-sided rhythmic jerking of the arm and head, with relative stillness of the left arm and no spread to the legs, and movements are synchronous. Additionally, there is one observation of generalized bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous jerking of arms and legs involving the entire body, with persistent unresponsiveness throughout.

The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by subtle head version and in another by lip smacking. This progresses to tonic stiffening involving the neck, upper limbs, and upper body, including bilateral arm extension and clenched fists. Subsequently, asynchronous or bilateral clonic jerks occur in the arms and legs. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by initial right arm flexion with left extension (fencer posture) and subtle head version. This rapidly progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements, characterized by tonic stiffening of the neck and upper limbs, then evolving into asynchronous clonic jerking of the arms and legs. The episode concludes with unresponsiveness, apnea, and the patient lying still.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle head version, progressing to tonic stiffening involving the neck and upper limbs, with bilateral arm extension, clenched fists, and head retraction. This is followed by a clonic phase characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking of the arms, face, extremities, and head. The seizure evolves into a post-ictal state marked by unresponsiveness and shallow breathing.

The patient begins with a blank stare, accompanied in one instance by lip smacking and subtle head version. The tonic phase progresses to bilateral clonic jerks affecting the arms, legs, and head, including rhythmic movements during the clonic phase. Clenched fists are observed during the tonic stiffening.

Tonic stiffening of the arms with clenched fists, followed by bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The patient exhibits fencer-like posturing, with either right arm flexion and left arm extension or vice versa, evolving into asynchronous shaking; rhythmic jerks occur subsequently, and the patient is unresponsive at the end.

The patient is initially lying down, looking at a mobile phone, with a blank stare and lip smacking. She then exhibits tonic stiffening of the neck and upper limbs, accompanied by right arm flexion and head version to the right. This progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking, involving the face, arms, and legs. The seizure evolves into a fencer-like posture with left arm extension and right arm flexion. Throughout the episode, the patient moves her arms and head, appears to be in distress, raises her arms and head, then lies back down while continuing to show motor agitation.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs, which evolves into bilateral clonic jerks with asynchronous shaking of the arms and legs. Some episodes include lip smacking and facial grimacing. The events progress to rhythmic limb jerking and conclude with gradual relaxation, though in some cases the patient becomes unresponsive before returning to baseline.

This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by asynchronous jerking that progresses to rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso, involving the entire body with synchronous convulsions and loss of responsiveness.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by subtle bilateral rhythmic jerking movements that evolve into more pronounced asynchronous clonic jerks involving the limbs and trunk, with side-to-side head movement. The episode concludes with the patient becoming unresponsive.

Blank stare, followed by right arm flexion with left extension (fencer) and rightward head version with right arm extension; subsequently, rhythmic jerks and bilateral tonic–clonic jerks occur. Unresponsive at the end.

The patient becomes unresponsive with clenched jaw and tongue protrusion at the end.

The patient is lying supine with eyes closed and initially appears unresponsive, exhibiting a blank stare and subtle head version or lip smacking. There is no visible tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, automatisms, or asymmetric posturing at the onset. The episode progresses to tonic stiffening of the upper body, followed by bilateral, synchronous, and rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The patient becomes unresponsive during the event and remains motionless throughout the clip.

The patient initially exhibited a blank stare and subtle head version, then suddenly fell forward and became unresponsive. This progressed to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of the limbs and torso. After the convulsions ceased, the patient remained limp and collapsed forward into the chair.

The patient exhibits a blank stare with bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, progressing to clonic jerks involving the arms and legs. The movements are bilateral, generalized, and sustained, with asynchronous and rhythmic characteristics in some instances and synchronous and rhythmic patterns in others, but without clear unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the episode.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version with right arm extension, progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic activity. The patient exhibits bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, followed by synchronous, generalized rhythmic clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, with no clear unilateral predominance.

No clear seizure activity is observed; the patient remains motionless with eyes closed throughout the recording and shows no visible signs of automatisms, tonic stiffening, or clonic jerks. Multiple events begin with a blank stare, followed by one of several evolving patterns: rightward head version with right arm extension progressing to bilateral tonic–clonic movements or jerks; right arm flexion with left arm extension in a fencer-like posture, followed by rhythmic jerking that spreads bilaterally; or isolated rhythmic jerking of the right arm and leg that generalizes to asynchronous bilateral involvement. In some cases, there is initial tonic stiffening of the upper body and limbs, evolving into bilateral clonic jerks with asynchronous shaking. All episodes are associated with unresponsiveness at the end.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rhythmic jaw movements, lip smacking, and tonic stiffening of the trunk, upper body, and limbs. This evolves into bilateral clonic jerks involving the arms and legs, with asynchronous shaking and rhythmic movements; head and limb movements are often asynchronous. In some episodes, the initial phase includes focal motor signs such as right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posture) or isolated rhythmic jerking of the right arm and leg, which may spread to become bilateral. Bilateral tonic–clonic movements occur with synchronous or asynchronous jerking, and the patient remains unresponsive throughout and at the conclusion of each episode.

The patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and either right arm flexion or extension. This evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking and tonic stiffening with bilateral clonic jerks. The seizure progresses to a post-ictal state with unresponsiveness and includes fencer posturing characterized by left arm flexion with right arm extension. Additional features include lip smacking at onset.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking at onset, followed by progression to bilateral tonic stiffening of the limbs, trunk, and neck. This tonic phase is associated with clenched jaw and tongue protrusion in some episodes. Clonic activity typically follows, manifesting as rhythmic jerking involving the face, arms, and legs, which may begin focally (e.g., in the right arm and leg) before generalizing. Head version or deviation, as well as eye deviation, frequently occurs during the clonic phase, most commonly to the right. Fencer posturing—characterized by unilateral arm flexion with contralateral extension—is observed during some episodes, occurring on both sides across different events. The seizures evolve into generalized bilateral tonic–clonic movements in all cases, with synchronous, symmetric motor activity and no consistent unilateral predominance. The patient remains unresponsive throughout each episode, with post-ictal unresponsiveness consistently noted.

The patient consistently exhibits a blank stare at onset, often accompanied by subtle lip smacking or automatisms. This initial phase is followed by focal motor signs including rightward head version with right arm extension, or asymmetric posturing in the form of fencer-like presentation—typically right arm flexion with left arm extension, though occasional right leg extension is noted. Tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs frequently develops, evolving into bilateral clonic jerks that are rhythmic and asynchronous, involving the face, arms, legs, and head. In some episodes, focal clonic jerking begins unilaterally (right arm and leg) before generalizing. All events progress to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by stiffened limbs, clenched fists, and generalized convulsions. The patient becomes unresponsive during the course of each episode, which concludes with no immediate recovery of awareness.

Blank stare observed in multiple episodes, followed by right arm flexion with left arm extension (fencer posturing) and progression to rhythmic jerks or bilateral tonic–clonic activity; one episode began with rightward head version and right arm extension before evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Additional observations include lip smacking preceding bilateral clonic jerks and tonic stiffening with fencer posturing (left arm flexion, right extension). Patient consistently unresponsive at the end of each episode. One episode showed no early signs; patient remained still with eyes closed, no motor activity or posturing, and appeared unresponsive throughout.

The patient begins with a blank stare, often accompanied by automatisms such as lip smacking and hand clenching. This evolves into tonic stiffening involving the upper body, trunk, and limbs, with generalized stiffening and clenched jaw; in some episodes, right arm flexion with left extension (fencer posture) is observed. The tonic phase transitions into bilateral clonic jerks affecting the face, arms, and legs, characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous shaking and marked flexion-extension movements of the head and upper body. Some episodes include synchronous clonic activity, while others display persistent asynchronous shaking. Automatisms, including lip smacking and fist clenching, accompany several events. The seizures conclude with unresponsiveness, followed by limpness or postictal immobility.

The patient exhibits a blank stare, followed by variable initial features including rightward head version, subtle head version, lip smacking, or tonic stiffening of the trunk and limbs. This progresses to unilateral manifestations such as right arm flexion or fencer posturing (left arm flexion with right extension), which may evolve into bilateral tonic–clonic movements. The clonic phase is characterized by rhythmic, asynchronous jerking involving the arms and legs, sometimes with synchronous whole-body involvement and no clear unilateral predominance. The episodes culminate in postictal unresponsiveness, preceded by gradual relaxation.

The patient begins with a blank stare and subtle lip smacking, evolving into unilateral right arm flexion and tonic stiffening of the right side, accompanied by head version to the right. This progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with asynchronous shaking or jerking, including a fencer-like posture with right arm flexion and left extension, followed by rhythmic jerks. Automatisms include repetitive hand movements and head turning. The patient becomes unresponsive during the event.
